chunk_summary: Rehabilitation in SAM includes sensory stimulation and emotional support to improve neurodevelopmental recovery, caregiver engagement, and prevent relapse. Structured routines, responsive feeding, play sessions, and addressing barriers like maternal depression and food insecurity are emphasized.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of sensory stimulation and emotional support in SAM rehabilitation.
Learn how to create structured daily routines for children with SAM during recovery.
Identify and address barriers to recovery, including maternal depression and food insecurity.
Recognize early markers of recovery, such as improved playfulness and interaction.
key_facts: Sensory stimulation and emotional support should begin once the child is clinically stable.
Structured play sessions (15–30 minutes, at least twice daily) with low-cost, age-appropriate toys are recommended.
Improving playfulness is an early marker of recovery and should be documented alongside intake and weight gain.
Barriers like maternal depression, food insecurity, and domestic violence should be screened and addressed.
Deterioration in activity is a danger sign, even if weight has not yet fallen.
reference_quote: Rehabilitation in SAM includes developmental and psychosocial care, because apathy, irritability, and caregiver distress can reduce intake, worsen defaulting, and delay neurodevelopmental recovery even when therapeutic feeding is technically correct.
document_key_topics: Emotional support for caregivers and children
Addressing barriers to recovery in SAM
